title: Apply: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Threat of Mob Violence
description: Apply your understanding the road to reforming the American government in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Threat of Mob Violence
Apply: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Threat of Mob Violence

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is James Madison’s 1787 letter to Edmund Randolph.**

Our situation is becoming every day more & more critical. No money comes into the federal Treasury. No respect is paid to the federal authority; and people of reflection unanimously agree that the existing [[☃ definition 1]] is [[☃ definition 2]] to its foundation. Many individuals of weight particularly in the Eastern district are suspected of leaning towards Monarchy. Other individuals predict a partition of the States into two or more [[☃ definition 3]]. It is pretty certain that if some radical amendment . . .  can not be devised and introduced that one or other of these revolutions, the latter no doubt, will take place. I hope you are bending your thoughts seriously to the great work of guarding [against] Both.

**Which event most supports Madison’s argument that the “existing Confederacy is tottering to its foundation”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the increase in mob violence
the lack of foreign allies
the failure of the Treaty of Paris
the conflicts over westward expansion

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is James Madison’s 1787 letter to Edmund Randolph.**

Our situation is becoming every day more & more critical. No money comes into the federal Treasury. No respect is paid to the federal authority; and people of reflection unanimously agree that the existing [[☃ definition 3]] is [[☃ definition 4]] to its foundation. Many individuals of weight particularly in the Eastern district are suspected of leaning towards Monarchy. Other individuals predict a partition of the States into two or more [[☃ definition 5]]. It is pretty certain that if some radical amendment . . .  can not be devised and introduced that one or other of these revolutions, the latter no doubt, will take place. I hope you are bending your thoughts seriously to the great work of guarding [against] Both.

**Which problem with the Articles of Confederation does Madison describe in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: Congress could not pass new laws, making it difficult to address the everyday problems of governance.
Several states wanted to return to British rule, as the United States government was seen as ineffective.
Congress lacked taxation powers and struggled to pay the debts caused by the Revolutionary War.
Several states wanted to secede from the Union after disagreements over westward expansion.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from William Grayson’s 1786  letter to James Madison.**

There has been some serious thoughts in the minds of some of the Members of Congress to recommend to the States the meeting of a general Convention, to consider an alteration of the [Articles of Confederation], & there is a motion to this effect now under consideration. It is contended that the present Confederation is utterly inefficient, and that if it remains much longer in its present State of [[☃ definition 1]] we shall be one of the most contemptible Nations on the face of the Earth: for my own part I have not yet made up my mind on the subject. 

I am doubtful whether it is not better to bear those ills we have than fly to others that we know not of. I am however in no doubt about the weakness of the [federal] Government; if it was weaker notwithstanding, it would answer if the States had power as in the United Netherlands the [federal government] is weak but the Individual States are strong. It is no wonder our Government should not work well, being formed on the Dutch model where circumstances are so materially different.

**What does Grayson think is wrong with the Articles of Confederation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The federal government is responsible for the national currency.
The federal government does not have enough power.
The state governments do not have the power to check the federal government.
The state governments each have different styles of government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from William Grayson’s 1786  letter to James Madison.**

There has been some serious thoughts in the minds of some of the Members of Congress to recommend to the States the meeting of a general Convention, to consider an alteration of the [Articles of Confederation], & there is a motion to this effect now under consideration. It is contended that the present Confederation is utterly inefficient, and that if it remains much longer in its present State of [[☃ definition 3]] we shall be one of the most contemptible Nations on the face of the Earth: for my own part I have not yet made up my mind on the subject. 

I am doubtful whether it is not better to bear those ills we have than fly to others that we know not of. I am however in no doubt about the weakness of the [federal] Government; if it was weaker notwithstanding, it would answer if the States had power as in the United Netherlands the [federal government] is weak but the Individual States are strong. It is no wonder our Government should not work well, being formed on the Dutch model where circumstances are so materially different.

**Which statement best summarizes Grayson’s argument in his letter?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The Articles of Confederation has given too much power to the national government.
The Articles of Confederation prioritizes strong state governments and should be preserved.
The Articles of Confederation needs to be amended to better resemble the Dutch version of democracy.
The Articles of Confederation has created an ineffective government and needs to be changed.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1780 letter to Joseph Jones.**

Certain I am that unless Congress speaks in a more [[☃ definition 1]] tone—unless they are vested with powers by the several States [[☃ definition 2]] to the great purposes of War—or assume them as matter of right; and they, and the states [[☃ definition 3]], act with more energy than they hitherto have done, that our Cause is lost. We can no longer [[☃ definition 4]] on in the old way. By ill-timing the adoption of measures—by delays in the execution of them—or by [[☃ definition 6]] jealousies, we incur enormous expenses, and derive no benefit from them—One state will comply with a [[☃ definition 5]] of Congress; another neglects to do it; a third executes it by halves; and all differ either in the manner, the matter, or so much in point of time that we are always working up hill. . . 


**Which problem with the Articles of Confederation does Washington identify in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.definition: able to make decisions firmly and quickly
not able to be justified or excused

choices.content: Congress cannot force the British to pay back the debts from the Revolutionary War.
Congress cannot create a national dollar, which has led to inflation in the states.
Congress does not have enough power to force the states to obey.
Congress does not have a way to amend the Articles of Confederation to give it more power.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1780 letter to Joseph Jones.**

Certain I am that unless Congress speaks in a more [[☃ definition 1]] tone—unless they are vested with powers by the several States [[☃ definition 2]] to the great purposes of War—or assume them as matter of right; and they, and the states [[☃ definition 3]], act with more energy than they hitherto have done, that our Cause is lost. We can no longer [[☃ definition 4]] on in the old way. By ill-timing the adoption of measures—by delays in the execution of them—or by [[☃ definition 5]] jealousies, we incur enormous expenses, and derive no benefit from them—One state will comply with a [[☃ definition 6]] of Congress; another neglects to do it; a third executes it by halves; and all differ either in the manner, the matter, or so much in point of time that we are always working up hill. . . 

**Which statement best summarizes Washington’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: able to make decisions firmly and quickly
not able to be justified or excused

choices.content: The states have no authority to challenge Congressional powers, leading to a tyrannical government.
The states do not obey Congressional orders, making the federal government ineffective.
The states are vulnerable to foreign invasion, and Congress does not have the power to protect them.
The states are amending the Articles of Confederation too often, making it difficult for Congress to govern.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from *Observations on the Plan of Government Submitted to the Federal Convention of May 28, 1787* by Charles Pinckney.**

It is to this [need for] unanimous consent [that] the depressed situation of the Union is undoubtedly [[☃ definition 1]]. Had the measures recommended by Congress and [[☃ definition 2]] to, some of them by eleven and others by twelve of the States, been carried into execution, how different would have been the [[☃ definition 3]] of Public Affairs? To this weak, this absurd part of the Government, may all our distresses be fairly attributed.


**Which statement best describes the argument Pinckney makes in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Getting all the states to agree to an amendment is too difficult and is a major weakness of the Articles of Confederation.
The Revolutionary War has left several states in debt, and Congress has no plan for how to pay off that debt.
There is disagreement among the states about whether they will follow the laws passed by Congress.
Getting each state to print their own money has led to a financial crisis that Congress cannot address.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from *Observations on the Plan of Government Submitted to the Federal Convention of May 28, 1787* by Charles Pinckney.**

It is to this [need for] unanimous consent [that] the depressed situation of the Union is undoubtedly [[☃ definition 1]]. Had the measures recommended by Congress and [[☃ definition 2]] to, some of them by eleven and others by twelve of the States, been carried into execution, how different would have been the [[☃ definition 3]] of Public Affairs? To this weak, this absurd part of the Government, may all our distresses be fairly attributed.

**According to Pinckney, what process did the states follow to add amendments to the Articles of Confederation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Four of the 13 states had to agree to an amendment.
Seven of the 13 states had to agree to an amendment.
Eleven of the 13 states had to agree to an amendment.
All 13 states had to agree to an amendment.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an  excerpt from an article published in the *Pennsylvania Gazette* in 1787.**

The year 1776 is celebrated (says a [[☃ definition 1]]) for a revolution in favor of *Liberty.* The year 1787, it is expected, will be celebrated with equal joy, for a revolution in favor of *Government*. . . . 

Every state (adds our correspondent) has its SHAYS, who, either with their pens—or tongues—or offices—are [[☃ definition 2]] to [[☃ definition 3]] what *Shays* attempted [[☃ definition 4]] with his sword. In one of the states, this [[☃ definition 5]] tries to persuade the people that it is dangerous to increase the powers of Congress:—In another, *he* denies the authority of the Convention to [[☃ definition 6]] our national grievances:—In a third, he whispers distrust, saying the states will not adopt the new frame of government—In a fourth, he says the state constitutions, and the officers who act under them, are of divine right, and can be altered by no human power—and of course considers all attempts to restore order and government in the United States as a “laughable” thing:—In a fifth, he opposes a general [[☃ definition 7]], and urges the division of the states into three smaller confederacies, that he may then more easily place himself at the head of one of them. 

The spirit and wickedness of Shays is in each of these principles and measures. Let Americans be wise. [[☃ definition 8]] and Shayism are nearly allied. They both lead to slavery, poverty, and misery.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: someone who communicates by writing; someone who reports news
a leader who seeks support by appealing to people’s emotions and prejudices
belief in preserving the traditional structures of British society or older social structures in general

choices.content: Those who seek to undermine the new Constitution are as dangerous to the United States as Shays and other rebels.
The issues that Shays’ Rebellion raised are not resolved under the new Constitution, so the states should refuse to ratify it.
There are opponents to the new Constitution in every state, and they should be arrested just like those who participated in Shays’ Rebellion.
The new Constitution is necessary, as its main purpose is to strengthen the government’s ability to deal with public riots like Shays’ Rebellion.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a letter published in the *Albany Gazette* in 1787.**

But what in the name of common sense is this Shays? A mere tool of faction—a puppet which some political [[☃ definition 1]] has play’d off upon the populace—an idol, void of [[☃ definition 2]] merit, to which a thoughtless multitude have bent the knee.—Supposing that he was taken and [[☃ definition 3]] today—how soon would the prevailing rage of excessive democracy—this fashionable contempt of government—of public and private faith, raise up another Shays, as [[☃ definition 4]] and [[☃ definition 5]] as the present! . . . 

Let no one suppose that I am an enemy to freedom—I am a friend to liberty, and to secure it [[☃ definition 6]] to the people, would wish to banish [[☃ definition 7]].—But let them know, that without a sacred regard to the laws—a [[☃ definition 8]] submission to authority—an impartial and sometimes a severe administration of justice—this invaluable jewel, this boasted liberty will be inevitably lost. . . 


**Which statement summarizes the author’s opinion of Shays’ Rebellion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: belonging to a thing by its nature
kept free from attacks or destruction

choices.content: It was a necessary action taken by patriots to highlight the flaws of the Articles of Confederation and the need for reform.
It revealed the rise of factions in the United States and the need for the government to eliminate political parties to rule properly.
It was an example of how consent of the governed is the basis for democracy in the United States and showed how people should express grievances.
It was not a legitimate uprising and showed how mob rule threatens democracy and inevitably leads to tyranny.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “To the Freemen of the United States,” an article published in the *Pennsylvania Gazette* in 1787.**

If the evils we have suffered, and the [[☃ definition 1]] we have incurred, have not been sufficient to [[☃ definition 2]] us to alter our federal government, there is one argument that should possess a weight with us, that should be irresistible. Mankind insensibly glides into a stable government. The rich and the poor soon grow tired of [[☃ definition 3]]. They prefer the order and tranquility of [[☃ definition 4]] to popular [[☃ definition 5]] . . . . Hence the success of [[☃ definition 6]] in every age and country. It [[☃ definition 7]] us, therefore, to prevent the power which is the offspring of force, by means of a regular constitution, founded in a mutual [[☃ definition 8]] between rulers and the people.


**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: government by a cruel leader with total power

choices.content: True peace is achieved only when the government has total control over the people it rules.
The chaos caused by Shays’ Rebellion proves that Americans cannot be trusted with a democracy.
The American people need to change their government to prevent a tyrannical government from taking control.
Because society will naturally move toward stability, there is no need to alter the current structure of the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “To the Freemen of the United States,” an article published in the *Pennsylvania Gazette* in 1787.**

I see no reason why a republic, composed of a legislature properly compounded and balanced, where representation is equal, and elections annual, should not continue to be the [[☃ definition 1]] of liberty to the end of time. We have, therefore, my fellow-citizens, no choice left to us. We must either form an efficient government for ourselves, suited in every respect to our [[☃ definition 2]] and interests, or we must submit to have one imposed upon us by accident or [[☃ definition 3]]. 

A [[☃ definition 4]] will exercise dominion over us, if we neglect any longer to choose a vine or a fig-tree for that purpose. The present relaxed state of government in America is no common temptation to ambition. A federal Shays may be more successful than the Shays of Massachusetts Bay, or a body of men may arise, who may form themselves into an order of hereditary nobility, and, by surprise or [[☃ definition 5]], [[☃ definition 6]] our liberties at their feet.

**Which statement best identifies the author’s recommendation in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: something that allows you to achieve a goal
urgent problems that require immediate action
throw down; cause to be flat on the ground

choices.content: To return the government to a monarchy in which members of the same family inherit power
To replace the government under the Articles of Confederation with a stronger government that can address the issues Americans face
To rise up and revolt against the United States in a rebellion like Shays’ Rebellion to highlight the failures of the federal government
To reduce the powers of the national government in order to protect the individual rights and liberties of Americans
